description:
A backup-operator wants a quick way to spot problems in a recent test-restore run.  
    All raw output of the restore engine has already been saved for you in  
    /home/user/logs/restore.log.  

    Your job is to create an *alert-only* view of that log by performing the
    steps below from the shell (≈ 3–4 commands in total is enough; use whatever
    tools you like, e.g. grep/awk/sed/cut).

    1. Read /home/user/logs/restore.log and pull out **only** the lines whose
       third pipeline-separated field is either WARN or ERROR.  
       • Keep the original line order.  
       • Do not change, trim, re-wrap, or otherwise alter the lines.

    2. Write the extracted lines to a **new** text file named  
       /home/user/logs/restore_alerts.log.  
       The file must be plain UTF-8 with Unix line-endings.

    3. When (and only when) all alert lines have been written, append **one
       completely blank line** followed by a *single* summary line whose exact
       format is:  

           SUMMARY: WARN=<N> ERROR=<M>

       – where <N> is the count of WARN lines you extracted and <M> is the
       count of ERROR lines you extracted.  
       – Make sure there are no extra spaces anywhere in that summary line
         beyond the single spaces shown above.

    4. As a courtesy to the operator, also print the summary line to STDOUT.

    The automated checker will verify that:

       • /home/user/logs/restore_alerts.log exists  
       • Its *first* non-blank lines are exactly the WARN/ERROR lines (no INFO
         lines, no duplicates, no header) in the same order as in the source
         log.  
       • There is exactly one completely blank line before the summary.  
       • The summary line matches the specified literal pattern.  

    Note: you do **not** need root privileges, and no new directories are
    required—only the single output file. Keep your command list short and use
    standard GNU tools available on a minimal Linux install.
truth:
Initial filesystem items (already present before the agent starts):

    1. Directory: /home/user/logs
       • Permissions: 755 (owner=user)
       • Contains the following file.

    2. File: /home/user/logs/restore.log  
       • Permissions: 644  
       • UTF-8, Unix line endings  
       • Exact contents (9 lines, shown here verbatim):

2023-09-10 12:45:27 | INFO  | Restore started for backup set ID=BK20230910A
2023-09-10 12:46:00 | WARN  | Missing incremental file inc-20230909-02.delta
2023-09-10 12:46:05 | INFO  | Attempting partial restore
2023-09-10 12:47:10 | ERROR | Restore failed with code ERR42
2023-09-10 12:47:45 | INFO  | Clean-up finished
2023-09-11 08:10:32 | INFO  | Restore started for backup set ID=BK20230911A
2023-09-11 08:10:55 | WARN  | File changed during restore: /var/lib/data/app.db
2023-09-11 08:12:23 | ERROR | Restore aborted due to checksum mismatch
2023-09-11 08:13:00 | INFO  | Sending failure report email

    ---------------------------------------------------------------
    Expected artefacts after successful task completion:

    3. File: /home/user/logs/restore_alerts.log  
       • Permissions should be 644 (but test suite will chmod if necessary).  
       • Exact contents (6 logical lines: 4 alert lines, 1 blank, 1 summary):

2023-09-10 12:46:00 | WARN  | Missing incremental file inc-20230909-02.delta
2023-09-10 12:47:10 | ERROR | Restore failed with code ERR42
2023-09-11 08:10:55 | WARN  | File changed during restore: /var/lib/data/app.db
2023-09-11 08:12:23 | ERROR | Restore aborted due to checksum mismatch

SUMMARY: WARN=2 ERROR=2

       • Note: there is *exactly one* empty line between the alert block and
         the SUMMARY line (no spaces, just “\n”).

    4. Runtime expectation: the agent also echoes the identical summary line
       (“SUMMARY: WARN=2 ERROR=2”) to STDOUT once, but the checker focuses on
       the file’s presence and correctness.
